policy_statement: Increase access to drinking water by making public drinking fountains easily accessible in dense areas and near every sports ground and children’s play area.
mechanism_description: Public drinking fountains will be made easily accessible in dense areas and near sports grounds and children’s play areas, improving access to drinking water and helping people stay hydrated during heat.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (extreme heat) → ↑ hydration access in public space → ↓ heat stress/health impacts (↓ vulnerability)
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: The city installs and maintains public drinking fountains in high-use areas (dense neighborhoods, sports grounds, play areas) to ensure free/easy access to potable water. (2) Mediating process: During hot conditions, improved availability of drinking water enables more frequent hydration and supports thermoregulation, reducing dehydration and heat strain. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower vulnerability to extreme heat, reducing heat-related morbidity/mortality and associated health-system stress.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced heat-related illness and mortality by lowering exposure/susceptibility during heat events through improved access to drinking water.
mechanism_classification_reasoning: 1) Dominant causal pathway: extreme heat risk is addressed by increasing access to drinking water, which reduces heat stress impacts—an adaptation (risk reduction) pathway. 2) Why this primary over alternatives: while fountains could marginally reduce bottled-water production/transport emissions, the described and generally largest climate-relevant benefit of this mechanism is protecting people during heat events. 3) Edge cases applied: if a policy explicitly framed fountains as reducing single-use plastics/embodied emissions, it could introduce a mitigation/resource-efficiency co-benefit, but that is not the dominant pathway for this mechanism as specified (heat resilience/hydration). 4) Applies to all instances: any policy deploying public drinking fountains to improve drinking water access for staying hydrated in heat functions primarily as heat adaptation by reducing vulnerability. 5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., public health resilience, heatwave response, equity focus) is deferred to policy-level evidence and wording.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential co-benefit: reduced bottled water consumption (resource efficiency/mitigation) if explicitly targeted; possible maladaptation considerations if water supply is scarce or energy-intensive (requires local context). Otherwise straightforward heat-adaptation mechanism.
key_indicators: "Increase access to drinking water"; "Public drinking fountains"; "easily accessible"; "densely built-up areas"; "immediate vicinity of every sports ground"; "children's play area"
co_benefits: Improved general public health and wellbeing; support for physical activity in public spaces; reduced single-use bottled water consumption and litter.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a planning/siting strategy (amenity siting standard), but the text reads primarily as a commitment to provide/install fountains (infrastructure deployment).
